infobox.value: [RGCCI](/wiki/Registrar_General_and_Census_Commissioner_of_India "Registrar General and Census Commissioner of India")

or the **15th [Indian census](/wiki/Census_of_India "Census of India")** was conducted in two phases, house listing and population enumeration. The House listing phase began on 1 April 2010 and involved the collection of information about all buildings. Information for [National Population Register](/wiki/National_Population_Register "National Population Register") (NPR) was also collected in the first phase, which will be used to issue a 12-digit unique identification number to all registered Indian residents by [Unique Identification Authority of India](/wiki/Aadhaar "Aadhaar"). The second population enumeration phase was conducted between 9 and 28 February 2011. Census has been conducted in India since 1872 and 2011 marks the first time biometric information was collected. According to the provisional reports released on 31 March 2011, the Indian population increased to 1.21 billion with a decadal growth of 17.70%.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Adult literacy rate increased to 74.04% with a decadal growth of 9.21%. The motto of the [census](/wiki/Census_of_India "Census of India") was *Our Census, Our Future*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Stamp_of_India_-_2011_-_Colnect_259244_-_Census_Of_India.jpeg/250px-Stamp_of_India_-_2011_-_Colnect_259244_-_Census_Of_India.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Stamp_of_India_-_2011_-_Colnect_259244_-_Census_Of_India.jpeg)

Postage stamp dedicated to the 2011 census of India

The **2011 census of India** or the **15th [Indian census](/wiki/Census_of_India "Census of India")** was conducted in two phases, house listing and population enumeration. The House listing phase began on 1 April 2010 and involved the collection of information about all buildings. Information for [National Population Register](/wiki/National_Population_Register "National Population Register") (NPR) was also collected in the first phase, which will be used to issue a 12-digit unique identification number to all registered Indian residents by [Unique Identification Authority of India](/wiki/Aadhaar "Aadhaar"). The second population enumeration phase was conducted between 9 and 28 February 2011. Census has been conducted in India since 1872 and 2011 marks the first time biometric information was collected. According to the provisional reports released on 31 March 2011, the Indian population increased to 1.21 billion with a decadal growth of 17.70%.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Adult literacy rate increased to 74.04% with a decadal growth of 9.21%. The motto of the [census](/wiki/Census_of_India "Census of India") was *Our Census, Our Future*.

Spread across 28 [states](/wiki/States_of_India "States of India")[[a]](#cite_note-preTG-3) and 8 [union territories](/wiki/Union_territories "Union territories"), the census covered 640 districts, 5,924 sub-districts, 7,935 towns and more than 600,000 villages. A total of 2.7 million officials visited households in 7,935 towns and 600,000 villages, classifying the population according to gender, religion, education and occupation.[[3]](#cite_note-pr_census_2011-4) The cost of the exercise was approximately ₹2,200 crore (US$260 million)[[4]](#cite_note-5) – this comes to less than US$0.50 per person, well below the estimated world average of US$4.60 per person.[[3]](#cite_note-pr_census_2011-4)

Information on [castes](/wiki/Caste_system_in_India "Caste system in India") was included in the census following demands from several ruling coalition leaders including [Lalu Prasad Yadav](/wiki/Lalu_Prasad_Yadav "Lalu Prasad Yadav"), and [Mulayam Singh Yadav](/wiki/Mulayam_Singh_Yadav "Mulayam Singh Yadav") supported by opposition parties [Bharatiya Janata Party](/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party"), [Shiromani Akali Dal](/wiki/Shiromani_Akali_Dal "Shiromani Akali Dal"), [Shiv Sena](/wiki/Shiv_Sena "Shiv Sena") and [All India Anna Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam](/wiki/All_India_Anna_Dravida_Munnetra_Kazhagam "All India Anna Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam").[[5]](#cite_note-6) Information on caste was last collected during the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") in 1931. During the early census, people often exaggerated their caste status to garner social status and it is expected that people downgrade it now in the expectation of gaining government benefits.[[6]](#cite_note-7) Earlier, there was speculation that there would be a caste-based census conducted in 2011, the first time in 80 years (last was in 1931), to find the exact population of the "[Other Backward Classes](/wiki/Other_Backward_Class "Other Backward Class")" (OBCs) in India.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) This was later accepted and the [Socio Economic and Caste Census 2011](/wiki/Socio_Economic_and_Caste_Census_2011 "Socio Economic and Caste Census 2011") was conducted whose first findings were revealed on 3 July 2015 by Union Finance Minister [Arun Jaitley](/wiki/Arun_Jaitley "Arun Jaitley").[[11]](#cite_note-12) Mandal Commission report of 1980 quoted OBC population at 52%, though National Sample Survey Organisation (NSSO) survey of 2006 quoted OBC population at 41%.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

There is only one other instance of a caste count in post-independence India. It was conducted in Kerala in 1968 by the [Government of Kerala](/wiki/Government_of_Kerala "Government of Kerala") under [E. M. S. Namboodiripad](/wiki/E._M._S._Namboodiripad "E. M. S. Namboodiripad") to assess the social and economic backwardness of various lower castes. The census was termed [Socio-Economic Survey of 1968](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Kerala#1968_Socio-Economic_Survey_of_Kerala "Ethnic groups in Kerala") and the results were published in the Gazetteer of Kerala, 1971.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_census_of_India&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

C. Chandramouli [IAS](/wiki/Indian_Administrative_Service "Indian Administrative Service") was the [Registrar General and Census Commissioner of India](/wiki/Registrar_General_and_Census_Commissioner_of_India "Registrar General and Census Commissioner of India") for the 2011 Indian census. Census data was collected in 16 languages and the training manual was prepared in 18 languages. In 2011, [India](/wiki/India "India") and [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") also conducted their first-ever joint census of areas along their border.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) The census was conducted in two phases. The first, the house-listing phase, began on 1 April 2010 and involved collection of data about all the buildings and census houses.[[16]](#cite_note-17) Information for the National Population Register was also collected in the first phase. The second, the population enumeration phase, was conducted from 9 – 28 February 2011 all over the country. The eradication of epidemics, the availability of more effective medicines for the treatment of various types of diseases and the improvement in the [standard of living](/wiki/Standard_of_living "Standard of living") were the main reasons for the high decadal growth of population in India.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### House-listings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_census_of_India&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: House-listings")]

The House-listing schedule contained 35 questions.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

1. Building number / *Census house number*
2. Predominant material of floor, wall and roof of the census house
3. Ascertain use of actual house
4. Condition of the census house
5. Household number
6. Total number of persons in the household
7. Name of the head of the household
8. Sex of the head
9. Caste status (SC or ST or others)
10. Ownership status of the house
11. Number of dwelling rooms
12. Number of married couples in the household
13. Main source of drinking water
14. Availability of drinking water source
15. Main source of lighting
16. Latrine within the premises
17. Type of latrine facility
18. Waste water outlet connection
19. Bathing facility within the premises
20. Availability of kitchen
21. Fuel used for cooking
22. Radio/Transistor
23. Television
24. Computer/Laptop
25. Telephone/Mobile phone
26. Bicycle
27. Scooter/Motor cycle/Moped
28. Car/Jeep/Van
29. Availing Banking services

### Population enumeration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_census_of_India&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Population enumeration")]

The Population enumeration schedule contained 30 questions.[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20)

1. Name of the person
2. Relationship to head
3. Sex
4. Date of birth and age
5. Current marital status
6. Age at marriage
7. Religion
8. Scheduled Caste/Scheduled Tribe
9. Disability
10. Mother tongue
11. Other languages known
12. Literacy status
13. Status of attendance (Education)
14. Highest educational level attained
15. Working any time during last year
16. Category of economic activity
17. Occupation
18. Nature of industry
19. Trade or service
20. Class of worker
21. Non economic activity
22. Seeking or available for work
23. Travel to place of work
24. Birthplace
25. Place of last residence
26. Reason for migration
27. Duration of stay in the place of migration

### National Population Register

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_census_of_India&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: National Population Register")]

The National Population Register household schedule contained 9 questions.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

1. Name of the person and resident status
2. Name of the person as should appear in the population register
3. Relationship to head
4. Gender
5. Date of birth
6. Marital status
7. Educational qualification
8. Occupation/Activity
9. Names of father, mother and spouse

Once the information was collected and digitised, fingerprints were taken and photos collected. [Unique Identification Authority of India](/wiki/Unique_Identification_Authority_of_India "Unique Identification Authority of India") was to issue a 12-digit identification number to all individuals and the first ID have been issued in 2011.[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)

## Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_census_of_India&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Population")]

The population of [India](/wiki/India "India") as per 2011 census was 1,210,854,977.[[31]](#cite_note-32) India added 181.5 million to its population since 2001, slightly lower than the population of [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"). India, with 2.4% of the world's surface area, accounts for 17.5% of its population. Uttar Pradesh is the most populous state with roughly 200 million people. Over half the population resided in the six most populous states of [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh"), [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"), [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar"), [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal"), [Andhra Pradesh](/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh_(1956%E2%80%932014) "Andhra Pradesh (1956–2014)") and [Madhya Pradesh](/wiki/Madhya_Pradesh "Madhya Pradesh").[[32]](#cite_note-33) Of the 1.21 billion Indians, 833 million (68.84%) live in rural areas while 377 million stay in urban areas.[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35) 453.6 million people in [India](/wiki/India "India") are migrants, which is 37.8% of total population.[[35]](#cite_note-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

India is home to many religions such as [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_India "Hinduism in India"), [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_India "Islam in India"), [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_India "Christianity in India"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_India "Buddhism in India"), [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") and [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"), while also being home to several indigenous faiths and tribal religions which have been practiced alongside major religions for centuries. According to the 2011 census, the total number of households in India is 248.8 million. Of which 202.4 million are Hindu, 31.2 million are Muslim, 6.3 million are Christian, 4.1 million are Sikh, and 1.9 million are Jain.[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40) According to 2011 census, there are around 3.01 million places of worship in India.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

## Religious demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_census_of_India&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Religious demographics")]

The religious data on India census 2011 was released by the Government of India on 25 August 2015.[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-Muslim_population_growth_slows-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49) Hindus are 79.8% (966.3 million) while Sikhs are 20.8 million comprising 1.72% of the population,[[49]](#cite_note-50) Muslims are 14.23% (172.2 million) in India[[47]](#cite_note-Muslim_population_growth_slows-48)[[50]](#cite_note-auto1-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52) and Christians are 2.30% (28.7 million). According to the 2011 census of India, there are 57,264 Parsis in India.[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54) For the first time, a "No religion" category was added in the 2011 census.[[54]](#cite_note-55) 2.87 million were classified as people belonging to "No Religion" in India in the 2011 census,[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57) 0.24% of India's population of 1.21 billion.[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59) Given below is the decade-by-decade religious composition of India until the 2011 census.[[59]](#cite_note-censusindia2011-60)[[60]](#cite_note-census1-61)[[61]](#cite_note-census2-62) There are six religions in India that have been awarded "National Minority" status – Muslims, Christians, Sikhs, Jains, Buddhists and Parsis.[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64) Sunnis, Shias, Bohras, Agakhanis and [Ahmadiyyas](/wiki/Ahmadiyya "Ahmadiyya") were identified as sects of Islam in India.[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67) As per 2011 census, six major faiths – Hindus, Muslims, Christians, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains make up over 99.4% of India's 1.21 billion population, while "other religions, persuasions" (ORP) count is 8.2 million. Among the ORP faiths, six faiths – 4.957 million-strong [Sarnaism](/wiki/Sarnaism "Sarnaism"), 1.026 million-strong [Gond](/wiki/Gondi_people "Gondi people"), 506,000-strong [Sari](/wiki/Sari_Dharam "Sari Dharam"), [Donyi-Polo](/wiki/Donyi-Polo "Donyi-Polo") (302,000) in Arunachal Pradesh, [Sanamahism](/wiki/Sanamahism "Sanamahism") (222,000) in Manipur, [Khasi](/wiki/Khasi_people "Khasi people") (138,000) in Meghalaya dominate.[[67]](#cite_note-68) Maharashtra is having the highest number of non-religious in the country with 9,652 such people, followed by Kerala.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

## Language demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_census_of_India&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Language demographics")]

Main article: [Languages of India](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Fastest_growing_languages_of_India_%E2%80%94_Hindi_%28first%29%2C_Kashmiri_%28second%29%2C_Gujarati_%26_Meitei_alias_Manipuri_%28third%29%2C_Bengali_%28fourth%29_%E2%80%94_based_on_2011_census_of_India.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fastest_growing_languages_of_India_%E2%80%94_Hindi_(first),_Kashmiri_(second),_Gujarati_%26_Meitei_alias_Manipuri_(third),_Bengali_(fourth)_%E2%80%94_based_on_2011_census_of_India.jpg)

Fastest growing [languages of India](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India") — [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") (first), [Kashmiri](/wiki/Kashmiri_language "Kashmiri language") (second), [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language") & [Meitei](/wiki/Meitei_language "Meitei language")/[Manipuri](/wiki/Manipuri_language "Manipuri language") (third), [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") (fourth) — based on 2011 [census of India](/wiki/Census_of_India "Census of India")[[70]](#cite_note-deccanherald.com-71)

The 2011 Census of India reported 121 languages having over 10,000 speakers, constituting 1.20 billion (~99%) of the population, consolidated from 1,369 classified and 1,474 unclassified forms out of 19,569 raw mother tongue entries recorded from respondents.[[71]](#cite_note-72) Out of 121 languages, 22 languages designated as [scheduled language](/wiki/Languages_with_official_recognition_in_India "Languages with official recognition in India") of India, which spoken by 96.71 percent of population as mother tongue.[[72]](#cite_note-73) The language data was released on 26 June 2018.[[73]](#cite_note-74) As per census, 57.1% of Indian population know Hindi,[[74]](#cite_note-fulllangdatacensus_2011-75) in which 43.63% of Indian people have declared Hindi as their native language or mother tongue.[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77) However, the census takes the widest possible definition of "Hindi" as a broad variety of "[Hindi languages](/wiki/Hindi_languages "Hindi languages")" which is widely spoken language in northern parts of India.[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79) [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") is the fastest growing [language of India](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India"), followed by [Kashmiri](/wiki/Kashmiri_language "Kashmiri language") in the second place, with [Meitei](/wiki/Meitei_language "Meitei language") (officially called [Manipuri](/wiki/Manipuri_language "Manipuri language")) as well as [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language"), in the third place, and [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") in the fourth place, according to the 2011 [census of India](/wiki/Census_of_India "Census of India").[[70]](#cite_note-deccanherald.com-71)

The 2011 census report on [bilingualism](/wiki/Bilingualism "Bilingualism") and [trilingualism](/wiki/Trilingualism "Trilingualism"), which provides data on the two languages in order of preference in which a person is proficient other than the mother tongue, was released in September 2018.[[79]](#cite_note-80)[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82) The number of bilingual speakers in India is 314.9 million, which is 26% of the population in 2011.[[82]](#cite_note-83) 7% of Indian population is trilingual.[[83]](#cite_note-84) Hindi, Bengali speakers are India's least multilingual groups.[[84]](#cite_note-85)

## Literacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2011_census_of_India&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Literacy")]

Any individual above age seven who can read and write in any language with an ability to understand was considered literate. In censuses before 1991, children below the age five were treated as illiterates. The literacy rate taking the entire population into account is termed as "crude literacy rate", and taking the population from age seven and above into account is termed as "effective literacy rate". Effective literacy rate increased to a total of 74.04% with 82.14% of the males and 65.46% of the females being literate.[[87]](#cite_note-88)
